Facts.text: Khan of the Uyghurs, Bögü Qaghan, conquers Luoyang, the capital of the Chinese Empire
Giovanni di Struma is elected antipope
Palermo, Sicily, is conquered by Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI
Pope Clement VI names John IV of Arkel as bishop of Utrecht
Cola di Rienzo, later Roman Tribunal, addresses a meeting of on the Capitol during people's revolt in Rome
Astronomer Regiomontanus returns with Cardinal Basilios Bessarion to his house in Rome, home to one of largest libraries in Europe
Friesland hit by heavy hail storm
Arabs attribute shortage of water in Jerusalem to Jews making wine
Duke of Parma conquers Aalst
Bishop Richelieu becomes French minister of Foreign affairs and War
Zumbi, the last King of the Quilombo dos Palmares in early Brazil and a former slave, is executed and decapitated, his head displayed on a pike to dispel any legends of his immortality
Sweden & Hannover sign for peace Treaty of Stockholm
Battle of Quiberon Bay begins: British win crucial naval victory over the French, thwarting a planned French invasion of Britain
Curacao government forbids slave work on Sunday
Russia, Prussia, Austria and England sign an Alliance "for the maintenance of peace in Europe" on the same day as the Treaty of Paris
Whaling ship Essex attacked and sunk by a sperm whale in the southern Pacific, only eight of the 20 crew men eventually survive (through cannibalism). Inspiration for the novel "Moby-Dick"
Jews expelled from Nikolayev and Sevastopol, Russia
Charles Reade and Tom Taylor's historical comedy play "Masks & Faces" premieres in London
Secession ordinance is filed by Kentucky's Confederate government
Confederate armies of Mississippi and Kentucky merge as Army of Tennessee, under General Braxton Bragg
First national convention of Grand Army of Republic (veterans' org)
French inventor Pierre Lallement patents rotary crank bicycle
Howard University founded in Washington, D.C.
Sherlock Holmes's first story "A Study in Scarlet" is accepted by publisher Ward and Lock with payment of £25
Willard Bundy patents timecard clock
US intervenes in Bluefields, Nicaragua
Jack Williams of Canadian football team Ottawa Rough Riders kicks 9 singles in a game
Gustav Mahler's "Das Lied von der Erde" (Song of the Earth) premieres in Munich, conducted by Bruno Walter
US State Department starts requiring photographs for passports
First successful use of a tank in battle at the Battle of Cambrai in World War I as Britain uses the new technology to break through German lines
Ukrainian Republic declared
1st municipally owned airport in US opens in Tucson, Arizona
Zoe Akins' play "Texas Nightingale" premieres in NYC
American inventor Garrett Morgan patents his traffic signal design, adding a caution between 'stop and 'go', an important development in automobile safety
Rentenmark replaces the Papiermark as the official currency of Germany at the exchange rate of one Rentenmark to One Trillion (One Billion on the long scale) Papiermark
WGH-AM in Newport News VA begins radio transmissions
First broadcast of "Goldbergs" on US radio
Commercial teletype service begins (AT&T)
Earthquake at Uden, Netherlands
New Belgian government of Theunis, Francqui & Gutt (3 bankers)
Toronto Maple Leafs left wing Busher Jackson becomes first NHL player to score 4 goals in a period in 5-2 win over St. Louis Eagles at St. Louis Arena
1st documented anti-semitic remarks over US radio (by Father Coughlin)
World War II: Hungary, Romania and Slovakia join the Axis Powers
Adm Nomura & Kurusu hands over Japanese last diplomatic note
German "auxiliary cruiser" (armed merchant raider) Kormoran sinks near Australia
26th Soviet Armoured Corps recaptures Perelazovski
British 8th Army recaptures Benghazi, Libya
NHL abolishes regular season OT until WW II is over
The Alaska Highway 2,451km long from Dawson Creek, British Columbia to Fairbanks, Alaska, first opens to military traffic
WWII: German submarine U-536 sunk northeast of the Azores in the Atlantic Ocean by British and Canadian warships
WWII: US forces land on Tarawa & Makin Atoll in Gilbert Island
1st Japanese suicide submarine attack (Ulithi Atol, Carolines)
Amsterdam: Vondelpark closed because of kappen of trees
Dutch Prince Bernhard establishes staff in Breda, Netherlands
Lillian Hellman's play "Another Part of the Forest" premieres in NYC
1st permanent TV installed on seagoing vessel (New Jersey)
UN General assembly begins debate on printing their own stamps
US balloon reaches height of 42.7 km (record)
Jewish population of Israel reaches 1,000,000
Snowdonia becomes a British National Park
Chicago Cubs slugging left fielder Hank Sauer wins NL MVP
George Axelrod's play "Seven Year Itch" premieres in NYC
Scott Crossfield in Douglas Skyrocket, 1st to break Mach 2 (1,300 MPH)
KTRK TV channel 13 in Houston, TX (ABC) begins broadcasting
Kripal Singh scores 100 on Test Cricket debut, India v NZ
Polly Umrigar scores India's 1st Test Cricket double century, 223 v NZ
UN adopts Universal Declaration of Children's Rights
WABC fires Alan Freed over his refusal to swear innocence in payola scandal (accepting bribes from record companies)
Australian driver Jack Brabham finishes 4th in season-ending US Grand Prix at Riverside Raceway to retain his F1 World Drivers Championship by 9 points from Bruce McLaren of New Zealand
WPLG TV channel 10 in Miami, FL (ABC) begins broadcasting
USSR agrees to remove remaining Ilyushin Il-28 bomber jets from Cuba, US lifts blockade
UN Security council calls for boycott of Rhodesia
WCNY TV channel 24 in Syracuse, NY (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dallas sacks Pittsburgh QBs an NFL record 12 times
Men in Zurich vote against female suffrage
At 11 AM, Census Clock at Department of Commerce ticks past 200 million
Farmington Mine Disaster: 78 miners are killed in a coal mining explosion in West Virginia, leading to the Federal Coal Mine Health and Safety Act of 1969
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Rookie Indian batsman Gundappa Viswanath scores 137 on Test Cricket debut v Australia in Kanpur
UN General Assembly votes on proposal to accept membership of People's Republic of China, measure fails to get two-thirds necessary to pass
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
2 British Army soldiers, William Watson (28) and James Strothers (31), are killed by a booby trap bomb set by the Provisional IRA in Cullyhanna, County Armagh, Ireland
Rangers' Jeff Burroughs wins AL MVP
The US files antitrust suit to break-up AT&T
Steve Largent begins NFL streak of 177 consecutive game receptions

Islamic extremists led by Juhayman al-Otaybi occupy the Grand Mosque of Mecca, declare the arrival of the Mahdi (the "redeemer of Islam") in the form of Mohammed Abdullah al-Qahtani and call for the overthrow of the House of Saud. Siege lasts two weeks with 100s killed and sets Saudi Arabia on an ultra-conservative path.
US's 1st artificial blood transfusion occurs at University of Minnesota Hospital
Steve Ptacek in Solar Challenger makes 1st solar-powered flight
United Artists withdraws $44 million movie "Heaven's Gate" for re-editing
Burundi adopts its constitution
El Salvador guerrilla group FMLN opens "limited offensive"
100 million watch ABC TV movie "The Day After" about nuclear war
Cleveland Browns shutout Patriots 30-0
NY Giants Butch Woolfolk ties NFL record of 43 attempts rushing
McDonald's makes its 50 billionth hamburger
SETI Institute (Search for ExtraTerrestrial Intelligence) is founded.
Microsoft Windows 1.0 is released
Afghanistan President Babrak Karmal flees
World Health Organization announces first global effort to combat AIDS
Boston College beats Army 38-24 in the 'Emerald isle Classic' at Dublin's Lansdowne Road Stadium, the first NCAA American Football game to be played in Europe
Milwaukee Brewers center fielder Robin Yount wins AL MVP
Sacramento Kings last NBA win on the road for over a year
Soviet Union shows reluctance to endorse the use of force against Iraq
US 68th manned space mission STS 38 (Atlantis 7) returns from space
Atlanta Braves Terry Pendleton wins NL MVP
Queen Elizabeth's home Windsor Castle catches fire
Jakovlev-42 crashes into mountain at Ohrid Macedonia, 116 killed
Savings and Loan scandal: The United States Senate Ethics Committee issues a stern censure of California senator Alan Cranston for his "dealings" with savings-and-loan executive Charles Keating.
Angolan government and UNITA rebels sign the Lusaka Protocol in Zambia, ending 19 years of civil war
Russian Kosmos satellites 2294, 2295 and 2296 launch
FDA approves new therapy for use as an initial AIDS treatment, 3TC
NASA Space Shuttle STS 74 (Atlantis 15), lands back at Kennedy Space Center
Iraq's Revolution Command Council formally endorses an agreement, arranged by Russia, that enables UN weapons inspection teams to resume operations in Iraq
Last original Florida Marlin, Jeff Conine, traded to KC Royals
Mavericks' A C Green sets NBA record of 907 consecutive games played
NY Islanders lose, beginning a 10 game losing streak
Yohannes Haile-Selassie discovers the partial skull of a 2.5 million year old human ancestor, confirming and establishing the new species Australopithecus garhi, in Bouri, Middle Awash, Ethiopia
First module of the International Space Station, Zarya, is launched
After the November 15 bombings, a second day of the 2003 Istanbul Bombings occurs in Istanbul, Turkey, destroying the Turkish head office of HSBC Bank AS and the British consulate.
After critical failures in the US financial system began to build up after mid-September, the Dow Jones Industrial Average reaches its lowest level since 1997

The Mexican government declares its economy technically out of recession after 2.93% GDP growth in the third quarter of 2009; Mexico had been in a severe economic crisis for over a year prior to its economic rebound
Toshiba unveils a robot designed to help in nuclear disasters
More than half of all trees in Amazon forest at risk of extinction according to data published in journal "Sciences Advances"
Atlanta's Georgia Dome, once the world's largest covered stadium, is destroyed in a controlled implosion
Investment corporation Tencent's market value hits $511 billion, - first Asian company to join the $500 billion club
Airbnb bans listings in Israeli settlements in the West Bank
Mississippi 15 week abortion ban overturned by US judge saying it violated women's constitutional rights
More than 40 religious scholars killed, at event to mark birth of prophet Mohammed, by a suicide bomber near the airport in Kabul, Afghanistan
Sell-off in technology stocks leads stock market losses wiping out all 2018 market gains
Britain's Prince Andrew announces he is stepping back from public duties after outcry from disastrous interview on his friendship with Jeffry Epstein
Oxford Dictionaries word of the year is "climate emergency"
Snakes lived with hind legs for 70 million years (Najash rionegrina), according to research from La Buitrera Palaeontological Area, Argentina, published in "Science Advances"
US Ambassador Gordon Sondland testifies in impeachment inquiry that “We followed the president’s orders.” and that “everyone was in the loop” over Ukraine dealings
New York state activates the National Guard to assist with historic snow event around Buffalo, with Orchard Park receiving almost 6 1/5 ft
UN COP27 summit agrees to establish fund to help poor and vulnerable countries with climate change, with committee to be made up of representatives from 24 countries
World's longest-serving president, authoritarian leader Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo, 80, wins re-election in Equatorial Guinea, extending his 43-year rule
Somalian authorities say 50 people have died in heavy rain and flooding, and 687,235 people displaced, amid large downpours across the Horn of Africa linked to El Nino
Woman suspected to be Thailand's worst-ever serial killer convicted and sentenced to death for poisoning a friend with cyanide, the first of 14 trials
Roman GeneralDiocletianis proclaimed emperor by the armies of the East and West after the death of Carinus at the Battle of the Margus
Edward Iis proclaimed King of England after the death of his father,Henry III, takes two years to return to England from the Ninth Crusade

A truce betweenJohn the Fearless, Duke of Burgundy and Louis of Valois, Duke of Orléans is agreed under the auspices of John, Duke of Berry. Orléans would be assassinated three days later by Burgundy.
English ministerWilliam Pitt the Elderresigns
Ludwig van Beethoven's "Fidelio", his only opera, premieres at Vienna's Theater an der Wien
Second Treaty of Paris: France has its borders reduced to those of 1790 and agrees to pay 700 million francs in indemnities to end theNapoleonic Wars
Gustav Mahlerpremieres his 1st Symphony at the Vigadó Concert Hall, Budapest, Hungary; it is not well received and he revises it several times before publishing 9 years later
PopeLeo XIIIencyclical "On Slavery in the Missions"
Dramatic opera "Grisélidis" byJules Massenet, Armand Silvestre and Eugène Morand premieres the Opéra-Comique in Paris, France
Geo Lefevre andHenri Desgrangecreate the Tour de France bicycle race
George Bernard Shaw's play "Doctor's Dilemma" premieres in London
Revolution breaks out in Mexico, led byFrancisco I. Madero
The funeral of Paul and Laura Lafargue (daughter ofKarl Marx) in Paris is attended byLenin; the two socialists died in a suicide pact in the belief that their political usefulness was at an end
Boston Gardens opens, Montreal Canadiens beat Boston Bruins, 1-0
Salvador Dali's first one-man show
Rolls Royceacquisition ofBentley Motorsis announced
17-year-old pitcher Eiji Sawamura gives up just 1 hit, aLou GehrigHR, as US All Stars beat Japan, 1-0
Lillian Hellman's "Children's Hour" premieres in NYC
Jose Antonio Primo de Rivera, founder of the Falange is killed by a republican execution squad
Queen Wilhelminaopens Dutch parliament in Hague
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Mediator Dei
Slánský trials begin - a series of Stalinist and anti-Semitic show trials in Czechoslovakia.
Morton Wishengrad's dramatic play "Rope Dancers", starringArt Carney, Siobhán McKenna, and Theodore Bikel, premieres at Cort Theatre, NYC; runs for 189 performances
American puppeteers Jim and Jane Henson establish Muppets, Inc. (now known as TheJim HensonCompany)
Mickey Mantlewins AL MVP for the 3rd time
Dmitri Shostakovich's 9th/10th String Quartet premieres in Moscow
Mets pitcherTom Seaver(16-12) is named NL Rookie of Year
Alcatraz Island off San Francisco is seized by militant Native Americans, inspiringWilma Mankillerto become involved
Brazilian soccer icon Pelé scores his 1,000th goal
San Francisco GiantWillie McCoveyedgesTom Seaveras NL MVP
Ronald Reaganannounces candidacy for Republican nomination for US President
George Harrisonappears on "Saturday Night Live": haggling withLorne Michaels; performing "Here Comes the Sun" and "Homeward Bound" withPaul Simon; and airing two music videos
Egyptian PresidentAnwar Sadatbecomes the 1st Arab leader to address Israeli Knesset
Walter Payton(Bears) rushes for NFL record 275 yards
United Nations Gulf War mediatorOlof Palmemakes first unsuccessful peace shuttle between Tehran and Baghdad
Ringo Starrreleases album "Stop and Smell the Roses" in the UK
RussianAnatoly Karpovretains world chess championship
Drew Barrymoreat age 7 hosts "Saturday Night Live"
"Terms of Endearment" from the book by Larry McMurtry, directed by James L. Brooks and starringShirley MacLaineand Debra Winger premieres in New York (Best Picture 1984)
Mets pitcherDwight Goodenwins NL Rookie of Year
New York YankeesDon Mattinglyeasily wins AL MVP
Argentine tennis starGabriela Sabatinibeats Pam Shriver 7–5, 6–2, 6–2 to win her first WTA Tour Championship at Madison Square Garden, NYC
British Prime MinisterMargaret Thatcherfails to defeat Michael Heseltine's bid for leadership of the Conservative Party
Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonwins AL MVP
Saddam Husseinannounces plans to release German hostages
Home crowd favoriteBoris Beckerclaims his 2nd ATP Tour World Championship tennis title beating AmericanJim Courier6–4, 6–3, 7–5 in the final in Frankfurt, Germany
Winnie Mandela's driver and bodyguard murdered in Johannesburg
American tennis starPete SamprasbeatsBoris Becker4–6, 6–3, 7–5, 6–4 to win his second ATP Tour World Championship title in Frankfurt, Germany
Unseeded Argentine tennis starGabriela Sabatiniclaims WTA Tour Championship with a 6-3, 6-2, 6-4 win over AmericanLindsay Davenportat Madison Square Garden, NYC

Apple Records releases The Beatles "Anthology 1" (double CD/triple LP), the first of a 3-part series of rare recordings and outtakes by the Beatles; set covers the years 1955-64, as well as new collaboration "Free as a Bird" - aJohn Lennondemo augmented by additional lyrics and performances by the surviving three; album tops Billboard chart in US and peaks at no. 2 in UK
Diana, Princess of Wales, admits she cheated onPrince Charlesin a TV interview
Philadelphia FlyersEric Lindrostries to bite San Jose Sharks defenseman Marty McSorley
Court in Taliban-controlled Afghanistan declares accused terroristOsama bin Laden"a man without a sin" in regard to the 1998 U.S. embassy bombings in Kenya and Tanzania
Josh Grobanreleases his debut album "Josh Groban"; sales exceed over 5 million units
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushdedicates the US Department of Justice headquarters theRobert F. KennedyJustice Building, on what would have been his 76th birthday
"Die Another Day", 20th James Bond film released, directed by Lee Tamahori, starringPierce BrosnanandHalle Berry: Brosnan's last turn in the role
David Nalbandian of Argentina outlasts defending championRoger Federer6–7, 6–7, 6–2, 6–1, 7–6 in Shanghai, China to win his only career season-ending Tennis Masters Cup title
Retired Washington Post journalist and editorBen Bradleeis presented with the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Barack Obama at a White House ceremony
US PresidentBarack Obamaposthumously awards civil rights activistBayard Rustinthe Presidential Medal of Freedom
Nearly 5 million illegal migrants in the US have the threat of deportation deferred, after PresidentBarack Obamaannounces sweeping immigration changes
XL/Columbia releases "25", 3rd studio album byAdele(Grammy Award for Album of the Year, 2017; 2016 Billboard Album of the Year); features the singles "Hello" and "Send My Love (To Your New Lover)"
ScotsmanAndy Murraybreaks through for a first season-ending ATP World Tour Finals tennis title with a 6-3, 6-4 win in London over 5-time defending championNovak Đoković
South Korean PresidentPark Geun-hye's controversial friend Choi Soon-sil charged with abuse of authority and coercion amid calls to also impeach the President
German ChancellorAngela Merkelannounces that coalition talks to form a government have collapsed
Indian actorAmitabh Bachchanconfirms he has paid off the debts of 1,398 farmers worth $560,000, amid Indian agricultural crisis
South African golferGary Playerreceives PGA recognition for his 3 Senior British Open wins (1988, 90, 97) from before event recognised as a major title (2003); takes him to 9 Senior majors
Adam Ondracompletes the first ascent of Zvěřinec in Czech Republic, claiming it as his hardest 9b+ and the second hardest route of his life
Disney announcesBob Iger's return as chief executive less than a year after he retired from the company

Dutch driverMax Verstappenretains Formula 1 Drivers' C'ship for Red Bull with a win in the season-ending Abu Dhabi GP; Verstappen's 3rd straight Abu Dhabi win, record 15th of the season, record 454 points for season
Elton Johncompletes the North American leg of his "Farewell Yellow Brick Road: The Final Tour" at Dodger Stadium in Los Angeles, California
Serbian tennis starNovak Đokovićwins record equalling sixth ATP World Tour Finals beating Casper Ruud of Norway 7-5, 6-3 in Turin, Italy
US PresidentJoe Bidenturns 80 years old, becoming first octogenarian to serve in country's highest office
World's tallest woman Rumeysa Gelgi, at 7ft 0.71 from Turkey, meets the world's shortestJyoti Amge, at 2ft 0.7in from India, at the Savoy Hotel, London, to celebrate Guinness World Records Day
New Jersey is 1st state to ratify Bill of Rights
TheNuremberg war trialsbegin as 24 Nazi leaders are put on trial before judges representing the victorious Allied powers
Maximinus II, Emperor of the Roman Empire (308-313), born in Felix Romuliana (Gamzigrad, Serbia) (d. 313)
Bassui Tokusho, Zen founder (Kogakuji monastery in Nakamura Japan), born in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan (d. 1387)

Abraham de Wicquefort, Dutch diplomat (for Brandenburg at the French court, 1626-58; for the Duchy of Braunschweig-Lüneburg at the Hague, 1660-75). and historian, born in Amsterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1682)
Peregrine White, First English child born in the Massachusetts Bay Colony, born on board the Mayflower (d. 1704)
Avvakum, Russian Orthodox archpriest and writer, born in Grigorovo, Nizhny Novgorod (d. 1682)
Artus Quellinus II, Flemish sculptor, born in Sint-Truiden, Limburg, Belgium (d. 1700)
Paulus Potter, Dutch painter, born in Enkhuizen, Dutch Republic (d. 1654)
Romanus Weichlein, Austrian composer, born in Linz, Austria (d. 1706)
Daniel Ernst Jablonski, German Protestant theologian, born in Nassenhuben [now Mokry Dwór, Poland] (d. 1741)
Oliver Wolcott, American judge, signed US Declaration of Independence, born in Windsor, Connecticut (d. 1797) [NS=Dec 1]
Thomas Chatterton, English poet (Christabel), born in Bristol, England (d. 1770)
Giovanni Battista Gaiani, Italian organist and composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1819)
Nikolaus Zmeskall, Hungarian diplomat, cellist, and composer, baptized in Leštiny, Árva County, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1833)
Pius VIII [Francesco Saverio Castiglioni], Italian 253rd Pope (1829-30), born in Cingoli, Marche, Papal States (d. 1830)
Friedrich Heinrich Himmel, German composer, born in Treuenbrietzen, Brandenburg, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1814)
Thomas Fremantle, British naval officer, born in Aston Abbotts, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1819)
John Wall Calcott, English composer, born in Kensington, London (d. 1821)
Karl Friedrich Eichhorn, German jurist, born in Jena, Germany (d. 1854)
James L. Lardner, American naval officer (American Civil War), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1881)
Edmond Dédé, Creole-American violinist, composer (Quasimodo Symphony; Mephisto Masque), and conductor, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1901)[1][2]
Patrick Henry Jones, Irish-American lawyer, Postmaster of New York City, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in County Westmeath, Republic of Ireland (d. 1900)
Franjo Ksaver Kuhač, Croatian composer who collected and published 1,600 folk songs, born in Osijek, Croatia (d. 1911)
John Thomas Croxton, American attorney, diplomat, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Paris, Kentucky, (d. 1874)
Lewis Waterman, American insurance agent and inventor of an improved fountain pen, born in Decatur, New York (d. 1901)[1]
William Painter, American inventor (crown cork bottle cap and opener), born in  Triadelphia, Maryland (d. 1906)
Christian Wilberg, German painter, born in Havelberg, Germany (d. 1882)
Victor D'Hondt, Belgian mathematician, born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1901)
James Murray Spangler, American inventor of portable vacuum cleaner (Hoover), born in Pennsylvania (d. 1915)[1]
Arthur Goring Thomas, English composer, born in Ratton Park, Sussex, England (d. 1892)
Margherita of Savoy, Queen consort of the Kingdom of Italy by marriage to Umberto I (1878-1900), born in Palazzo Chiablese, Turin, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1926)
Josiah Royce, American philosopher (Conception of Immortality), born in Grass Valley, California (d. 1916)
Willem Vliegen, Dutch journalist and chairman (SDAP), born in Gulpen, Netherlands (d. 1947)
Gerard Wilhelm Kernkamp, Dutch historian and editor (Groene Amsterdammer), born in Hoorn, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Vicente Ripollés, Spanish composer, born in Castellón de la Plana, Spain (d. 1943)
William Heard Kilpatrick, American mathematician and philosopher, born in White Plains, Georgia (d. 1965)
Daniel Gregory Mason, American composer (Chanticker), born in Brookline, Massachusetts (d. 1953)
James Michael Curley, American politician (53rd Governor of Massachusetts, 1935-37), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1958)
Franz Pfemfert, German journalist and writer, born in Lötzen, East Prussia (d. 1954)
George McBride, American baseball shortstop (AL fielding leader 1912-15 Washington Senators) and manager (Washington Senators 1921), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1973)
Arthur Marshall, American ragtime piano player and composer ("Swipesy Cakewalk" with Scott Joplin; "The Peach"), born in Saline County, Missouri (d. 1968)
Andy Coakley, American baseball pitcher (Philadelphia A's, Cincinnati Reds) and coach (Williams College 1911–13, Columbia University 1915–18), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1963)
Norman Thomas, American socialist, pacifist and presidential candidate (1928-48), born in Marion Ohio (d. 1968)
Karl von Frisch, Austrian ethologist and bee expert (Nobel 1973), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1982)
Robert Armstrong, American actor (Mighty Joe Young, King Kong), born in Saginaw, Michigan (d. 1973)
Julian Fuhs, German-American jazz pianist and bandleader (Follies Band), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1975)
Leon Cadore, American baseball pitcher (pitched all of 26 inning game), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1958)
Reginald Denny, English actor (Rebecca, Cat Ballou, Batman), born in Richmond, Surrey, England (d. 1967)
James Collip, Canadian biochemist and physiologist (insulin), born in Belleville, Ontario (d. 1965)
Carl Mayer, Austrian-Jewish actor (Dreaming Lips, Ariane, Sunrise), born in Graz, Austria (d. 1944)
Yevgenia Ginzburg, Russian writer, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1977)
Lores Bonney, Australian aviator, 1st female solo flight Australia to UK, born in Pretoria, South Africa (d. 1994)
Margaret Sutherland, Australian composer (Haunted Hills; The Young Kabbarli), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 1984)
Juan Vicente Lecuna, Venezuelan diplomat and composer, born in Valencia, Venezuela (d. 1954)
Chester Gould, American cartoonist (Dick Tracy), born in Pawnee, Oklahoma (d. 1985)
Wolfgang Kunkel, German lawyer and law historian, born in Fürth, Germany (d. 1981)
Ishtiaq Hussain Qureshi, Pakistani historian and academic, born in Patyali, near Allahabad, United Provinces of British India (d. 1981)
Alexandra Danilova, Russian-American ballerina (The Turning Point), born in Petergof, Russia (d. 1997)
Fran Allison, American actress (Kukla, Fran & Ollie), born in La Porte City, Iowa (d. 1989)
Henri-Georges Clouzot, French director (Le salaire de la peur), born in Niort, France (d. 1977)
Alistair Cooke, British-American TV host (Masterpiece Theatre), born in Manchester, England (d. 2004)
Alan Bible, American lawyer and politician (Sen-D-Nev, 1954-74), born in Lovelock, Nevada (d. 1988)
Pauli Murray, African American poet and civil rights activist, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1985)
Willem Jacob van Stockum, Dutch physicist and mathematician, born in Hattem, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Jean Shiley, American high jumper (Olympic gold 1932), born in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1998)
Otto von Habsburg, last crown prince of Austria-Hungary and MEP (1979-1999), born in Wartholz Castle, Reichenau an der Rax, Lower Austria, Austria-Hungary (d. 2011)
Irina Nijinska, Russian-Polish-American ballet dancer, and teacher, born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1991)
Judy Canova, American comedian and actress (Cannonball), born in Starke, Florida (d. 1983)
(Clifton) "Skeeter" Best, American jazz guitarist (Erskine Hawkins; Earl Hines; Oscar Pettiford; Ray Charles; Milt Jackson), born in Kinson, North Carolina (d. 1985)
Emilio Pucci, Italian fashion designer (Neiman-Marcus Award-1954), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1992)
Kon Ichikawa, Japanese director (Matatabi, Money Talks), born in Ise, Mie, Japan (d. 2008)
Evelyn Keyes, American actress (Adventure of Martin Eden), born in Port Arthur, Texas (d. 2008)
Ferdinand Alphons Marie van der Ham, Dutch WW II resistance fighter, born in Voorburg, Zuid-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1944)
John Elwyn, British painter, born in Adpar, Wales (d. 1997)
Robert A. Bruce, American cardiologist and pioneer (exercise cardiology), born in Somerville, Massachusetts (d. 2004)
Max Georg Baumann, German composer (Libertas Cruciata), born in Kronbacher, Germany (d. 1999)
Pam Henning [Cornelia Catharina Pamela Ingenegeren], Indonesian-Dutch cabaret dancer and actress, born in Batavia, Dutch East Indies (d. 2008)
Ram Gopal, Indian-English dancer (Blue Peter, Purple Plain), born in Bangalore, India (d. 2003)
Robert Byrd, US Senator (Democrat-WV, 1959-2010), majority leader and once the longest-serving Senator in history, born in North Wilkesboro, North Carolina (d. 2010)
Dora Ratjen, German man who posed as a female high jumper (Olympic 4th 1936), born in Erichshof, Bremen, Germany (d. 2008)
Tibor Frešo, Slovak composer (Fanfares of Peace; A Beetle was Born), and conductor (Slovak National Theatre Opera), born in Spišský Štiavnik, Slovakia (d. 1987)
Dulcie Gray, British singer, actress, author and lepidopterist (Mine Own Executioner), born in Kuala Lumpur, Malaya (d. 2011)
Halka Grossman, Israeli resistance fighter and politician, born in Białystok, Second Polish Republic (d. 1996)
John McCarthy, English conductor, born in London (d. 2009)
Armin Schibler, Swiss composer (Devil in the Winter Palace), born in Kreuzlingen, Switzerland (d. 1986)
Douglas Dick, American actor and psychologist (Rope, Waterfront), born in Charleston, West Virginia (d. 2015)
Pernell Charity, American blues guitarist, singer, and songwriter, born in Waverly, Virginia (d. 1979)
Daniel "Dan" Frazer, American actor (Kojak, Take the Money and Run), born in New York City (d. 2011)
Jim Garrison, American district attorney, politician and judge, born in Denison, Iowa (d. 1992)
Phyllis Thaxter, American actress (Nora, Fort Worth), born in Portland, Maine (d. 2012)
Louis van Gasteren, Dutch actor and director (The House, De prijs van overleven), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2016)
Beryl Sprinkel, American economist (Council of Economic Advisers), born in Richmond, Missouri (d. 2009)
Nadine Gordimer, South African author (July's people, Nobel 1991), born in Springs, Transvaal, (d. 2014)
